coredata.dc:title: Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Bourlinos, Athanasios B.
Trivizas, Georgios
Karakassides, Michael A.
Baikousi, Maria
Kouloumpis, Antonios
Gournis, Dimitrios
Bakandritsos, Aristides
Hola, Katerina
Kozak, Ondrej
Zboril, Radek
Papagiannouli, Irene
Aloukos, Panagiotis
Couris, Stelios
coredata.dc:description: Microwave heating of an aqueous solution of citric acid, boric acid and urea affords boron-doped carbon dots (C-dots) with ultrafine size (2–6nm) and quasi-spherical shape. The new dots are freely dispersible in water providing stable, optically transparent dispersions with olive-green color. The optical properties of the dots were studied in respect to their photoluminescence and nonlinear optical response. Boron-doped C-dots exhibit absorption–emission features that strongly resemble the optical properties of conventional quantum dots with a strong blue fluorescence under UV excitation. Importantly, we demonstrate for the first time that boron doping in C-dots causes a significant enhancement of the nonlinear optical response compared to un-doped carbon dots. This result is in line with previous studies reporting a similar effect on the non-linear optical properties of boron-doped silicon nanocrystals.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support by the project LO1305 of the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic and the Operational Program Education for Competitiveness CZ.1.07/2.3.00/20.0155 of the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic. I.P, P.A and S.C. acknowledge the European Union (European Social Fund–ESF) and Greek national funds through the Operational Program ‘Education and Lifelong Learning’ of the National Strategic Reference Framework (NSRF)-Research Funding Program: THALIS (ISEPUMA) and HERAKLEITUS II: Investing in knowledge society through the European Social Fund. The Student Projects IGA_PrF_2014023 of Palacky University is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top: AFM topography image of CBU dots deposited on a Si-wafer (section analysis along the line in the image and depth analysis are included). Bottom: AFM image of a single dot along with the corresponding height profile (3nm). (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS spectrum of boron-doped carbon dots CBU: (a) survey XPS spectrum with atomic content. (b) High-resolution C 1s spectrum. (c) High-resolution N 1s spectrum. (d) High resolution B 1s spectrum. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical images of green-colored CBU dots (boron-doped) versus brown-colored C-dots (without boron) obtained by the microwave treatment of the citric acid-boric acid-urea and citric acid-urea systems, respectively. Top: the crude solids obtained just after the microwave treatment. Bottom: the corresponding green and brown aqueous dispersions of the dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

“Divided” (a and b) and “open-aperture” (c and d) Z-scans of a CBU dots water dispersion measured under 532nm, 35ps (left) and 4ns (right) laser excitation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Nonlinear optical parameters of boron-doped carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties

Bourlinos); Regional Centre of Advanced Technologies and Materials, Faculty of Science, Department of Physical Chemistry, Palacky University, Olomouc 77146, Czech Republic (Radek Zboril).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microwave heating of an aqueous solution of citric acid, boric acid and urea affords boron-doped carbon dots (C-dots) with ultrafine size (2–6nm) and quasi-spherical shape. The new dots are freely dispersible in water providing stable, optically transparent dispersions with olive-green color. The optical properties of the dots were studied in respect to their photoluminescence and nonlinear optical response. Boron-doped C-dots exhibit absorption–emission features that strongly resemble the optical properties of conventional quantum dots with a strong blue fluorescence under UV excitation. Importantly, we demonstrate for the first time that boron doping in C-dots causes a significant enhancement of the nonlinear optical response compared to un-doped carbon dots. This result is in line with previous studies reporting a similar effect on the non-linear optical properties of boron-doped silicon nanocrystals.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) is an important class of fluorescent nanoparticles that hold great promise in bioimaging and sensing applications in place of metal-containing quantum dots or fluorescent dyes [1–3]. C-dots generally consist of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen, have quasi-spherical shape (<10nm) and display multicolor emission [4–6]. Interestingly, doping with other heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulfur [7–10] or metal ions (e.g., Zn, Gd, Si) [11–13] may further alter the optical properties of the dots or even add new functionalities. To our knowledge, there are only few reports on boron-doped C-dots, mainly pertaining to their synthesis and sensing/bioimaging applications [14–17]. Likewise nitrogen and sulfur, boron can be as well incorporated into carbon dots through covalent bonds thus acting as an effective dopant. Other examples of boron-doped carbon materials include graphene, carbon nanotubes, fullerene and diamond [18–23]. In all instances, the substitutionally incorporated boron may alter the electronic and optical properties of the carbon matrix.

So far, the incorporation of boron into C-dots has been achieved by hydrothermal/solvothermal routes, arc-discharge in the gas phase or electrochemically [14–17]. Nevertheless, no report in the literature refers to the synthesis of boron-doped C-dots by the greener and faster proceeding microwave method. Recently, it has been demonstrated that the microwave heating of an aqueous solution of citric acid and urea leads to water-soluble luminescent C-dots of dark-brown color [24]. In a variation of this method, we show here that boron-doped C-dots can be simpler obtained from the microwave heating of an aqueous solution of citric acid, boric acid and urea, i.e. boric acid is just added to the citric acid-urea system. Citric acid serves as the carbon source whereas boric acid and urea as the boron and nitrogen sources, respectively. Both urea and boric acid react with citric acid to form polymeric precursors [24,25]. This allows a uniform molecular blending of all constituents into a single precursor. The obtained water-soluble nanodots (2–6nm, quasi-spherical) provide optically transparent dispersions with a distinct olive-green color. The optical properties of the dots were studied in respect to their photoluminescence and nonlinear optical response. Regarding photoluminescence (PL), boron-doped C-dots are strongly fluorescent in the blue region upon UV excitation showing absorption–emission spectra quite analogous to inorganic quantum dots. Furthermore, the nonlinear optical properties of the dots were studied by Z-scan technique employing 35ps and 4ns, visible and infrared laser pulses. The boron-doped carbon dots were found to exhibit significantly higher nonlinear optical response than other similar carbon dots without boron, e.g., some hydrophilic C-dots recently studied. The enhancing effect of boron on the nonlinear optical properties of the carbon dots is discussed and compared with other literature reports concerning doped silicon nanocrystals or quantum dots.

For the synthesis of the boron-doped C-dots (or CBU dots) 1g citric acid, 1g boric acid and 1g urea were dissolved in 20mL water. The solution was filtered off and the clear filtrate was heated for 4min in a domestic microwave oven (700W). The resulting solid was dissolved in 20mL water and the olive-green solution was filtered off. The clear solution was collected and evaporated until dry for 3days at room temperature. The dark green solid was rinsed several times with ethanol in order to remove impurities and re-dissolved in water. The as-obtained aqueous colloid was filtered off, dialyzed against pure water (0.5kDa cut off membrane) and collected for further analysis. The dispersed boron-doped C-dots have a characteristic olive green color and give the Tyndall effect with a laser pointer. Solid samples were obtained from the aqueous dispersion by water evaporation (note that the dispersed nanoparticles do not precipitate by polar organic solvents like acetone, methanol, ethanol, isopropanol, tetrahydrofuran or dimethylformamide). CBU dots have only a small negative surface charge (ζ =−10mV) probably due to the presence of charged borate ester bridges [26]. Even so, the dots are highly dispersible in water forming optically transparent dispersions that remain stable for an infinite time. The high solubility and colloidal stability of CBU dots in water can be ascribed to a rich content of hydrophilic surface groups like –OH and –NH2. The boron content was found 3% w/w based on ICP-MS analysis.

FT-IR spectra of samples in powder form, dispersed in KBr pellets, were measured with a Perkin–Elmer 210 GX, Fourier transform spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with the PHI VersaProbe II (Physical Electronics) spectrometer using Al Kα source (15kV, 50W). The obtained data were evaluated with the MultiPak (Ulvac – PHI, Inc.) software. The binding energy (BE) values were referenced to the C1s peak at 284.80eV. AFM images were obtained in tapping mode with a Multimode Nanoscope 3D using RTESP n-doped silicon cantilevels. For the AFM measurements, isolated dots deposited on a Si-wafer were obtained by a modified technique which combines Langmuir-Schaefer (LS) deposition and self-assembly as described elsewhere [27]. HR-TEM images were obtained on a Titan3 60–300 FEI microscope. Light Scattering was performed with Nano ZetaSizer Malvern Instrument equipped with a 4mW He–Ne laser, operating at a wavelength of 633nm, with which scattered light is measured at an angle of 173°. Instead of the hydrodynamic diameter, the derived count rate of scattered photons was recorded due to the very small size of the sample and inability to be properly probed with DLS. The derived count rate is the mean count rate normalized for the cell position and the attenuator used and is a function of both the number of the scatterers and their size. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer Hitachi F2500, and UV–Vis spectra with U-2800 Digilab Hitachi. The ICP-MS was measured with Agilent 7700× spectrometer, the exact amount of the sample was decomposed using nitric acid and the calibration was made using boronic acid as a standard.

The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of the CBU dots have been investigated by the Z-scan technique [28], which allows for the simultaneous determination of the sign and the magnitude of both the nonlinear refractive index parameter γ′ and the nonlinear absorption coefficient β of a sample. From these parameters, the real and the imaginary components of the third-order susceptibility χ (3) were calculated using the following relations [29]:

e

T

0.812 π
I

( 1 - S )
0.25
where L eff is the effective sample thickness, α 0 is the linear absorption coefficient at the excitation wavelength λ, L is the sample length, I 0 is the laser peak irradiance on the focal plane, z 0 is the Rayleigh length, z is the position of the sample, ΔTp
–
v is the difference between the peak and the valley of the normalized transmittance and S is the linear transmittance of the aperture, defined as S= 1−exp(−2ra
2/wa
2), with ra being the radius of the aperture and wa being the beam radius at the aperture. In the present study, a Q-switched 4ns and a mode-locked 35ps Nd:YAG laser systems have been employed, delivering 1064 and 532nm laser pulses, both operating at a repetition rate of 1–10Hz. Z-scan measurements have been performed both under visible (i.e., 532nm) and infrared (i.e., 1064nm). UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of the boron-doped C-dots dispersions were recorded before and after laser irradiation in order to confirm their stability upon laser exposure. The samples were put in 1mm path quartz cells.

AFM images of CBU dots deposited on a Si-wafer are shown in Fig. 1 . Relatively uniform, nearly spherical particles void of any aggregation can be seen. The height of these particles is between 2 and 6nm, as derived from topographical height profile (section analysis) while the average height is 3.7nm as resulted from depth profile analysis. HR-TEM of a single dot shows a crystalline core consisting of parallel crystal planes with lattice spacing of d 002 =3.1Å (see Fig. S1 in Supporting Information). Generally, B-doped graphite shows smaller d 002 values than un-doped graphite (3.35Å) [30]. For confirmation of colloidal/material properties of the CBU sample Light Scattering was performed. In Fig. S2, the mean derived count rate in kilocounts per second is plotted for three control samples (distilled water, citric acid and poly(ethylene glycol), Mw=750Da) and compared with CBU. The much higher number of scattered photons for the case of CBU is evidence that the size of the basic structural units of this material is well beyond that of small molecules.

The FT-IR spectrum of the CBU sample is given in Fig. S3 in Supplementary Information. Broad absorption bands at 3100–3500cm−1 are assigned to O–H and N–H. Absorption bands at 1600–1750cm−1 are assigned to carbonyl CO groups. These functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the dots in water [24]. The present spectrum shares similar features with pyrolytic boron carbide derived from a boric acid-PVA polymeric precursor [31]. The strong absorption at 1450cm−1 is attributed to B–O stretching vibration and B–C absorption band appears at 1190cm−1. The bonding of B–O–C is supported by the absorption peak at 1030cm−1. The spectrum shows additional bands due to B–N bonding (urea serves as the nitrogen source) [32]. For sp2-bonded BN there are two characteristic peaks at 1360 and 790cm−1, which are associated with the in-plane B–N bond stretching vibration and the out-of-plane B–N–B bending vibration, respectively. BN with sp3 bonding, on the other hand, has an IR active TO (transverse optical) mode at 1100cm−1.

The incorporation of boron into the structure of CBU was also confirmed by XPS analysis (Fig. 2 ). The sample contains 66.5at.% of carbon, 22.9at.% of oxygen, 8.2at.% of nitrogen and 2.4at.% of boron at corresponding binding energies given in Fig. 2. The content of boron is also in accordance with the result obtained by ICP-MS (3%). The high resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows the presence of carboxylic groups as well as CC and C–O/C–N bonds. The presence of B–N bond is also apparent from high resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c). The high resolution B 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) also revealed the presence of B–O and B–C bonds confirmed by FT-IR.

Aqueous dispersions of CBU dots have a characteristic olive-green color that deviates from the dark-brown dispersions obtained by C-dots (Fig. 3 ). Similar green color has been also observed for electrochemically derived boron-doped graphene quantum dots [17]. The absorption spectrum of the dots in water shows a well-defined UV band at 350nm which possibly represents the typical absorptions of an aromatic π system (Fig. S4 in Supporting Information) [24]. Accordingly, excitation at 350nm gives an emission band at 450nm corresponding to blue PL (Fig. S4 in Supporting Information). Bright PL under UV light is shown as inset (QY: 10–15%). This distinct absorption–emission spectrum resembles the optical behavior of established inorganic quantum dots such as CdTe, InP and BN [33–35]. One of the common features found in C-dots is the excitation-dependent emission shown in Fig. S4B. When the excitation wavelengths changed from 250 to 350nm, the fluorescence peak had no obvious shift. However, for excitation wavelengths >350nm, the fluorescence peak of CBU dots shifted to longer wavelengths and the fluorescence intensity decreased remarkably, as it is common for most C-dots [36]. Time-resolved PL measurements (Fig. S5 in Supporting Information) indicate that CBU dots show decay lifetimes ranging between τ =5–10ns, depending on the excitation wavelength (e.g., τ =9.63ns for λ ex =350nm, λ em =450nm and τ =5.27ns for λ ex =420nm, λem=540nm). Such fluorescence lifetimes are consistent with C-dots [37].

The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of the CBU dots have been studied by the Z-scan technique. Fig. 4 shows some representative “divided” (Fig. 4a) and “open-aperture” (Fig. 4c) Z-scans of a 0.58mgmL−1 CBU dispersion in distilled water, obtained under 532nm, 35ps laser excitation conditions. As shown, the “divided” Z-scans of the CBU dispersion and of the neat water exhibited a pre-focal transmission valley followed by a post-focal transmission peak, indicative of self-focusing effect (i.e., positive sign γ′). However, since the variation of the transmission (ΔTp
–
v ) of the CBU sample was found to be lower than that of water, the latter possessing positive γ′, it can be safely concluded that boron-doped carbon dots possess opposite sign γ′, i.e., negative. In addition, as depicted from the practically flat “open-aperture” Z-scans (Fig. 4c), both CBU dispersion and water were found to exhibit negligible nonlinear absorption under ps laser pulses (for the range of intensities employed). On the other hand, the CBU dispersions were found to exhibit significant nonlinear absorption under ns excitation (Fig. 4d) while the water did not have any NLO response. In particular, the “divided” Z-scans of the CBU dispersions exhibited a pre-focal transmission maximum followed by a post-focal transmission minimum, indicative of negative sign Reχ (3), corresponding to a self-defocusing. Additionally, the corresponding ns “open-aperture” Z-scan exhibited transmission minimum, corresponding to a reverse-saturable absorption (RSA) response. Measurements performed under infrared 35ps and 4ns laser pulse have not shown any NLO response. From several Z-scan recordings, performed for various incident laser intensities (e.g., see Fig. S6 in Supporting Information), the values of the NLO parameters and the third-order nonlinear susceptibility χ (3) have been determined and shown in Table 1 for the two excitation regimes. For comparison purposes, the NLO parameters of un-doped carbon dots (GU5) in water are also included in Table 1. GU5 dots have been previously obtained by microwave heating of a gallate-urea system in water and contain no boron [38]. As a figure of merit of the nonlinear optical response of the carbon dots, the ratio χ (3)/C, where C is the concentration, has been calculated and is also given. As can be seen, boron doping of the C-dots resulted in significant enhancement of the NLO response compared to the un-doped carbon dots (GU5). In particular, in the case of 35ps laser excitation a 25-fold enhancement was observed while a 4-fold enhancement was determined in the case of 4ns laser excitation.

Carbon dots exhibit similar properties with quantum dots (e.g., semiconducting behavior, PL, etc). Therefore, the origins of their nonlinear optical response can be considered at first approximation to be similar to those of their inorganic counterparts. In fact, several studies have reported that the NLO properties of quantum dots can be enhanced through doping. So, the nonlinear refraction and absorption properties of silicon quantum dots or nanocrystals have been reported that they can be enhanced several times through doping by boron or phosphorus [39–41]. Similarly, the efficient enhancement of the three-photon absorption cross-section of ZnSe/ZnS quantum dots through doping by manganese has been reported [42]. A similar enhancement of two-photon absorption coefficient of ZnSe quantum dots through Mn+2-doping has been reported elsewhere [43], while lastly, the enhancement of the nonlinear absorption of ZnS nanoparticles through doping by copper has been also reported [44].

Regarding the effect of the doping on the optical properties of dots, it should be noted that doping is employed in semiconductors in order to modify their electronic properties [42], and it is associated with the formation of new electronic states. Such new electronic states can alter the optical properties as well. In this way, the photoluminescence of quantum dots can be enhanced or spectrally shifted by doping [44,45]. In particular, for silicon nanocrystals doping with boron or phosphorus has been proposed to create impurity-related electronic states just below the conduction band [46]. The new states can result in higher linear absorption leading to enhancement of the NLO response [41].

It should be also noted that the boron-doped C-dots (CBU) presented in this work contain nitrogen because of the urea precursor (8.2% following XPS analysis). For instance, microwave treatment of a citric acid-urea system results in nitrogenous C-dots (see elemental analysis in [24], N: ∼20% w/w). On the other hand, the GU5 reference sample (boron-free) is obtained by a microwave treatment of a gallate-urea system leading also to nitrogen-doped C-dots (see elemental analysis in [38]; N: 18% w/w). Therefore, boron-doped C-dots and GU5 reference are both of nitrogenous character. In this respect, the difference observed between the NLO responses of these two systems can be ascribed to boron doping of CBU.

In summary, we have synthesized new boron-doped C-dots from microwave heating of a citric acid-boric acid-urea aqueous solution. The dots have ultra-fine size (2–6nm), quasi-spherical shape and are highly dispersible in water affording clear olive green aqueous dispersions. CBU dots show excitation dependent fluorescence similar to other C-dot systems but with a remarkably strong blue fluorescence under UV excitation. The nonlinear optical response of the boron-doped carbon dots was found to be larger than that of un-doped hydrophilic carbon dots. Boron-doped C-dots might have potential uses in cell labeling, photodynamic therapy or various photonic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support by the project LO1305 of the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic and the Operational Program Education for Competitiveness CZ.1.07/2.3.00/20.0155 of the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic. I.P, P.A and S.C. acknowledge the European Union (European Social Fund–ESF) and Greek national funds through the Operational Program ‘Education and Lifelong Learning’ of the National Strategic Reference Framework (NSRF)-Research Funding Program: THALIS (ISEPUMA) and HERAKLEITUS II: Investing in knowledge society through the European Social Fund. The Student Projects IGA_PrF_2014023 of Palacky University is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.11.032.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document contains supplementary Figs. S1–S6.
